ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a congee/porridge class (FOODON_03315671). No specific entry for claypot frog porridge exists; congee class used as the closest defensible anchor. Frog leg component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice porridge/congee) used as the closest defensible anchor for the porridge base. No composite code exists for claypot frog porridge with braised frog legs.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for claypot frog porridge or frog leg congee as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of claypot frog porridge in Singapore is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of plain rice congee (approximately 250 g) and a claypot portion of braised frog legs in savory sauce (approximately 150 g).

nutrient_source.title: Frog Porridge — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for frog legs (raw) and rice congee
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo database used as primary source for Singapore-context nutrient values for frog porridge. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for frog legs (raw, NDB 15244) and plain rice congee used to cross-validate and supplement micronutrient values. Per-100g values represent the composite dish (porridge + braised frog legs) as typically served. Input enriched_json values are consistent with HPB/USDA cross-reference and are retained with medium-high confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice congee / plain rice porridge analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for claypot frog porridge as a composite dish. GI of 68 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain rice congee/porridge (GI ~68–78, mean ~72 for white rice congee). The presence of high-protein frog legs and savory sauce moderates the overall glycemic response of the meal; a conservative estimate of GI 68 is applied for the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 56.0) / 100 = 38.1 g (400 g serving × 14.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 56.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dark soy sauce and oyster sauce in the braised frog leg preparation (350 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1,400 mg per 400 g serving, approximately 61% of the 2,300 mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce, avoid consuming the concentrated braising liquid remaining in the claypot, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake to below 600 mg per meal where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic index (estimated GI 68) from the rice congee base, which undergoes extensive cooking that gelatinizes starch and accelerates glucose absorption; partially offset by the high-protein frog leg component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the congee portion to approximately 150–200 g per meal, prioritize the frog leg protein component to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response, as the glycemic load per standard serving (~38 g) is clinically significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium content raises blood pressure risk; however, frog meat is lean (low saturated fat at ~0.6 g per 100 g) and provides high-quality protein, which is broadly favorable for cardiovascular health when consumed without excess sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the ginger-scallion preparation over the Gong Bao style to reduce sodium and chili load, request minimal sauce, and limit serving frequency to avoid cumulative sodium excess.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Frog meat is lean and anti-inflammatory ginger is a key ingredient; however, dried chilies (nightshades) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis can consume this dish in moderation; those sensitive to nightshades should request the ginger-scallion variant without dried chilies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Claypot Frog Porridge (田鸡粥, Tiánjī zhōu) is a beloved late-night supper dish in Singapore and Malaysia, rooted in Cantonese culinary tradition. Despite its name, it is served as two distinct components: a bowl of smooth, thick, plain rice congee (porridge) and a separate claypot of braised frog legs. The frog legs are most commonly prepared in a Gong Bao (Kung Pao) style — braised in a rich, savory, slightly sweet sauce made from dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, ginger, garlic, and dried chilies — or alternatively in a milder ginger-and-scallion style. The diner spoons the frog legs and their aromatic sauce over the congee, creating a harmonious contrast of textures: silky, comforting porridge against tender, succulent frog meat. Frog meat is widely described as having a mild, chicken-like flavor and texture. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~110 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~8.5 g per 100 g) from the lean frog legs, and moderate carbohydrates (~14 g per 100 g) from the rice congee base. Sodium is elevated (~350 mg per 100 g) due to the soy-based braising sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 68 (medium) for the composite dish, with the protein component moderating the glycemic response of the high-GI congee base. The dish is not suitable for strict vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets without modification. It is a culturally significant comfort food, commonly enjoyed as a late supper at hawker centres and zi char restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in claypot frog porridge?
Is frog porridge high in sodium?
What does frog meat taste like?
What sauce is used in frog porridge?
Where to eat frog porridge in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values are sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore database cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for frog legs and rice congee components. No single approved-source entry exists for the exact composite dish (claypot frog porridge with Gong Bao sauce); values represent an adapted composite estimate consistent with both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for rice congee. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite nature of the dish and absence of a single exact approved-source entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the composite dish (plain rice congee + braised frog legs in Gong Bao sauce) as typically served in Singapore hawker centres, estimated at a 60:40 ratio of congee to frog leg preparation by weight. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (claypot frog porridge) is not directly reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024; neither source provides a moisture value for the combined congee-plus-braised-frog-leg dish, and estimating water content from components would introduce unacceptable uncertainty given the variable sauce-to-porridge ratio. 3. saturated_fat_g (0.6 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5 g), and cholesterol_mg (35 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy frog legs (raw, NDB 15244) and rice congee components, adjusted for the braising sauce contribution. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with the absence of hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation. 5. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as it is factually incorrect for a dish containing frog meat. 6. The duplicate portion entry in the input has been deduplicated to a single 400 g serving. 7. Halal status is marked false as frog is not universally accepted as halal under all Islamic jurisprudence schools (Shafi'i school considers frog haram); consumers should verify with their religious authority. 8. GI glycemic load per serving: (68 × 56.0 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 38.1 g GL.
